coredata.dc:title: Hand-in-hand structured DNA monolayer for dual-mode analysis of circulating tumor DNA
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guangxing
Chai, Hua
Guo, Zhenzhen
Wang, Zhaoyin
Tang, Yuguo
Miao, Peng
coredata.dc:description: DNA nanostructures with different functions have been extensively studied and applied. Controllable and programmable assembly of functional DNA nanostructures still requires in-depth investigation to guarantee accurate and reliable practical utility. In this study, we have extended the conception of common molecular beacon for the fabrication of novel DNA nanostructures. Three hairpin fuel strands are designed for target circulating tumor DNA triggered construction of three-way junction structures. By the conjugation of swing arms, hand-in-hand DNA monolayer is achieved at the electrode surface. On one hand, electrochemical species could be located near electrode interface due to this structure for electrochemical output; on the other, the hand-in-hand pattern could be cleaved after the addition of DNAzyme cofactor and the released strands carry abundant carbon nanodots for fluorescent out. With the dual-mode analysis, highly selective and reliable detection of target is achieved. The high sensitivity of the proposed method is attributed to the recycling of target strands during the formation of multiple three-way junctions. It holds great potential for the application of DNA analysis and clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: circulating tumor DNA
Three-way junction
Hand-in-hand DNA structure
Strand displacement reaction
Carbon nanodots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon nanodots and DNA conjugation
Preparation of Probe A modified electrode
Reproducibility, selectivity and practical utility investigations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern of carbon nanodots. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of carbon nanodots in the absence and presence of Ag+. (C) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the carbon nanodots at excitation wavelengths progressively increased from 360 to 560 nm. (D) Photostability of carbon nanodots. Effect of (E) salt concentration and (F) pH on fluorescence emission of carbon nanodots.
(A) Nyquist plots and (B) cyclic voltammograms corresponding to bare electrode, Probe A modified electrode, after interaction with ctDNA, Probe B, Probe C and Mg2+ in succession.

(A) Square wave voltammograms of bare electrode and DNA modified electrode before and after reacted with 1 pM target ctDNA. (B) Square wave voltammograms for the detection of ctDNA with the concentrations of 10-16, 10-15, 10-14, 10-13, 10-12, 10-11, 10-10 M. (C) The linear relationship between logarithm of ctDNA concentration and peak current intensity. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of bare carbon nanodots, solutions after incubated with electrode in the absence and presence of 1 pM target ctDNA. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra for the detection of ctDNA with the concentrations of 10-16, 10-15, 10-14, 10-13, 10-12, 10-11, 10-10 M. Inset shows the fluorescence image of corresponding reacted solutions dropped on mica. (F) The linear relationship between logarithm of ctDNA concentration and decreased fluorescence peak intensity.

(A) SWV peak intensities, (B) decreased fluorescence peak intensities and (C) normalized ratios for mismatched sequences and target ctDNA induced reactions. (D) Normalized ratios for the detection of ctDNA in PBS and serum conditions.
Illustration of the hand-in-hand strategy for electrochemical sensing of target ctDNA.

head.ce:title: Hand-in-hand structured DNA monolayer for dual-mode analysis of circulating tumor DNA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou 215163, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou 215163, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, People’s Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: DNA nanostructures with different functions have been extensively studied and applied. Controllable and programmable assembly of functional DNA nanostructures still requires in-depth investigation to guarantee accurate and reliable practical utility. In this study, we have extended the conception of common molecular beacon for the fabrication of novel DNA nanostructures. Three hairpin fuel strands are designed for target circulating tumor DNA triggered construction of three-way junction structures. By the conjugation of swing arms, hand-in-hand DNA monolayer is achieved at the electrode surface. On one hand, electrochemical species could be located near electrode interface due to this structure for electrochemical output; on the other, the hand-in-hand pattern could be cleaved after the addition of DNAzyme cofactor and the released strands carry abundant carbon nanodots for fluorescent out. With the dual-mode analysis, highly selective and reliable detection of target is achieved. The high sensitivity of the proposed method is attributed to the recycling of target strands during the formation of multiple three-way junctions. It holds great potential for the application of DNA analysis and clinical diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a complex group of diseases, most of which remain incurable [1,2]. Cancer is one of the leading causes of death worldwide. The poor survival rates are mainly ascribed to the late stage diagnosis and limited treatments [3,4]. Cancer biomarkers present in tumor tissues or body fluids give valuable information about current and future behaviors of cancer, which can be used for cancer risk indication, early disease diagnosis, tumor classification and therapy monitoring [5–7]. A wide variety of molecules are encompassed such as DNA, RNA, enzymes, cell surface receptors and so on [8–10]. Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) carries tumor-specific sequence alteration, which is present in cell-free fraction of blood. It shows many merits over other traditional invasive markers [11,12]. Ultrasensitive analysis of ctDNA is not only helpful for diagnosis, but also beneficial to the monitoring of treatment response, which is critical to avoid ineffective therapy and side effects [13]. Advances in sequencing technologies enables rapid identification of ctDNA [14]. However, sequencing requires complicated instruments and tedious procedures. Currently, various biosensors have been developed for ctDNA including electrochemical assay [15], optical sensor [16], mass spectrometry [17], and so on. For example, Zhou et al. fabricated a surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensor for ctDNA analysis assisted by single-walled carbon nanotubes [18]. Hun et al. developed a self-powered photoelectrochemical sensor based on co-regulation of electron acceptors coupled with recombinase polymerase amplification [19]. Koo et al. designed magnetic-assisted bioelectrocatalytic cycling of DNA-intercalating and freely diffusing redox probes for electrochemical detection of ctDNA [20]. Nevertheless, most of current approaches still suffer from drawbacks like insufficient sensitivity, accuracy or selectivity. Continuous efforts should be made for the development of novel biosensors [21].

Duo to the definite base pairing property and controllable rigid-flexible feature of DNA strands, various DNA nanostructures can be developed for nanofabrication applications including biosensing purposes [22,23]. Molecular beacon is a typical functional DNA nanostructure which is usually a stem-loop hairpin structured oligonucleotide containing target binding domain [24]. Conventional molecular beacon is dual-labeled with a reporter dye at one end and a quencher at the other [25]. With target recognition and signal transduction capabilities, molecular beacon has been widely used in a range of biological assays including quantitative PCR [26]. However, in most cases, the generated signal comes from a 1:1 stoichiometric recognition event between target and molecular beacon, which limits the improvement of sensitivity.

In this report, we extend the conception of molecular beacon and fabricate a dual-mode biosensor for ctDNA combing electrochemical and fluorescent signals. Three functional hairpin-structured probes are involved, which are labeled with independent groups for the purposes of interface immobilization and signal outputs. Target induced DNA conformational changes trigger step-by-step assembly of the three hairpins, initiating automatic production of hand-in-hand structured DNA monolayer. This unique structure enriches abundant DNA strands at the electrode surface and combines dual-mode signals for further analysis. Compared with other designs, the operation herein is quite facile without any tedious reactions. In addition, target strand is recycled in this process, which significantly helps the improvement of sensitivity. A few inputs of ctDNA could initiate remarkable signal variations. By analyzing the electrochemical and fluorescence emission responses, an ultrasensitive biosensor for target ctDNA is achieved with self-calibration and high reliability.

Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), magnesium chloride, ethylenediamine, dichloromethane, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), o-phenylenediamine, ammonium acetate and mercaptohexanol (MCH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Human serum samples were supplied by local hospitals (China). Informed written consent from all participants was obtained prior to the research. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Double-distilled water was used throughout the experiments, which was purified with a Millipore system at a resistivity of 18 MΩ cm. All oligonucleotides were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China) with the sequences listed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon nanodots and DNA conjugation
ce:section.ce:para: Red emissive carbon nanodots were prepared via a facile microwave strategy. Briefly, 0.54 g of o-phenylenediamine and 0.385 g of ammonium acetate were mixed in 2.5 mL of pure water. Afterward, 0.5 mL of H2SO4 (98 %) was added. The mixture was exposed to microwave heat for about 5 min. The resulted solid product was then dissolved in DMF, followed by the addition of ethylenediamine for neutralization. In order to purify carbon nanodots, the product was extracted by the use of dichloromethane. After three cycles’ washing by ethanol, carbon nanodots were collected and then dried.

The further conjugation of Probe B was as follows. 10 µg of carbon nanodots, 1 mg of EDC, 1 mg of NHS were mixed in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). The final concentration of carbon nanodots was set to be 10 µg mL−1. After blending for 0.5 h, 20 µL of Probe B was added and the mixture was kept stirring for 100 min. Then, it was stored at 4 °C for 12 h. To obtain purified Probe B-carbon nanodots conjugation, ultrafiltration using YM-30 ultrafilter was performed and the obtained product was redissolved in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Probe A modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Substrate gold electrode was firstly pretreated with piranha solution (30 % H2O2 and 98 % H2SO4 = 3: 1) for 5 min (Caution: Piranha solution was highly dangerous!). After that, it was polished using P5000 silicon carbide paper and alumina powders (1, 0.3, 0.05 μm), successively. Then, the electrode was carefully rinsed and incubated with 50 % HNO3 for 30 min. Next, it was electrochemically cleaned using 0.5 M H2SO4. After rinsing, the cleaned electrode was incubated with 0.3 mL of Probe A (0.8 μM, 10 mM Tris-HCl, 10 mM TCEP, 0.1 M NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, pH 7.5) for 9 h. A further treatment of MCH (1 mM) was performed to achieve a well aligned DNA monolayer.

A series of concentrations of target ctDNA solutions were firstly prepared, which were then mixed with Probe B and Probe C. The final concentration of Probes B/C was 1.2 μM. After incubation for 100 min, multiple three-way junctions were formed and conjugated to form the hand-in-hand monolayer. Electrochemical species were located at the electrode interface for analysis.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out by the use of an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660D, CH Instruments) at room temperature. A traditional three-electrode system was employed, which was consisted of the DNA modified gold working electrode, the saturated calomel reference electrode, and the platinum auxiliary electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out with the electrolytic solution of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 1 M KNO3. Square wave voltammetry (SWV) was performed in 20 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4). The parameters were as follows: 0.21 V biasing potential, 5 mV amplitude, and 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz frequency range for EIS; 0.6 to −0.2 V scan range, 50 mV/s scan rate for CV; −0.6 to 0 V scan range, 70 Hz frequency for SWV.

The hand-in-hand structured DNA monolayer modified electrode was immersed in the solution of 30 mM Mg2+ (0.3 mL) for 0.5 h. Afterward, the resulted solution was transferred in the cuvette for fluorescence measurement by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). In addition, one drop of the solution was casted on the surface of a mica and fluorescence image was taken by the large sight field fluorescence imager equipped with a 530 nm laser.

The assembly process of DNA nanostructures is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Three hairpin probes are designed to own sequences which can form multiple hybrids with each other under certain conditions. They are endowed with corresponding functions including electrode anchoring and dual-mode signal outputs. The first hairpin, Probe A, is immobilized at the gold electrode surface via the thiol group labeled at its 5′ terminal [27]. In the presence of target ctDNA, complete double strands are formed between target and its binding domain in Probe A. Meanwhile, the stem region is opened and a single-stranded region is released which is responsible to open the second hairpin of Probe B. It is previously conjugated with red emissive carbon nanodot through the 3′ terminal NH2 [28]. Subsequently, Probe C labeled with methylene blue (MB) is introduced. Its hairpin structure is opened by the released single-stranded region of Probe B. In addition, Probe C is able to displace target sequence to form a complete three-way junction structure. The target strand is thus recycled and assist the formation of multiple three-way junctions. Since abundant MB molecules at the 3′ terminal of Probe C are located near the electrode interface, significant electrochemical response could be recorded to reveal target ctDNA. In addition, the released single-stranded swing arm formed by 3′ terminal of Probe A and 5′ terminal of Probe B on the three-way junction act as DNAzyme coupling 3′ terminal of Probe B on adjacent three-way junction as the substrate. Hand-in-hand structured DNA monolayer is thus fabricated. With the existence of Mg2+, the substrate sequence can be cleaved and the conjugated carbon nanodots are released into the solution. By measuring the fluorescence emission intensity, original ctDNA level can also be evaluated by fluorescence technique. Combining target recycling mechanism and ratiometric analysis, accurate and ultrasensitive detection of ctDNA could be achieved for practical utility.

Carbon nanodots are a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials with several advantages for biosensing, including bright emission intensity, excellent biocompatibility, light bleaching resistance and ease of bioconjugation [29,30]. Herein, we have synthesized red emissive carbon nanodots from o-phenylenediamine and ammonium acetate via microwave treatment. The morphology is firstly investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The produced carbon nanodots are well dispersed with uniform distribution (Fig. S1A and S1B). The peaks in energy dispersive X-ray spectrum demonstrate the existence of NH2, SH, and OH groups in the carbon nanodots (Fig. S1C). N element is from o-phenylenediamine and ammonium acetate, while S element is from H2SO4, which participates in the synthesis process. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis is then performed. The broad peak around 3368 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching of OH and NH. The peaks at 1212 cm−1, 1342 cm−1, 1457 cm−1, 2934 cm−1 reflect CS, CN, CN, CH, respectively (Fig. S2A). The functional groups have then been quantified by the XPS technique and four significant peaks (O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p) are reflected in the survey spectrum (Fig. S2B). Detailed high-resolution survey scans are shown in Fig. S2C-S2F. The broad XRD peak at around 24° is also consistent with the previously reported reference about carbon nanodots (Fig. 1 A) [31]. We have then measured UV–vis absorption spectrum of bare carbon nanodots. As shown in Fig. 1B, two distinct absorption bands can be observed around 300 and 550 nm, respectively. However, after treated with silver ions (Ag+), the band around 550 nm disappears, suggesting that Ag+ might quench the fluorescence of carbon nanodots.

To fully investigate the fluorescence properties of the prepared carbon nanodots, excitation and emission spectra are recorded. As shown in Fig. 1C, excitation independent emission wavelength is determined to be 620 nm. The quantum yield is measured to be 36 % by FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Fig. S3). After compared with other reported red emissive carbon nanodots, the fluorescent property of the prepared nanomaterials in this work shows significant superiority (Table S2), which benefits the performances of the following fluorescent sensing. In addition, the fluorescence emission is quite stable in the conditions of long-term excitation, high salt and extreme pH environments (Fig. 1D to 1F). We have then tested the effect of metal ions on fluorescence emission of carbon nanodots. It is clearly that only Ag+ could significantly quench the prepared carbon nanodots and the emission intensity is closely related to the concentration of Ag+ (Fig. S4). The mechanism is that the surface groups of carbon nanodots is conjugated with Ag+, creating the e-/h+ pair. Due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle, non-radiative emission is resulted. However, Ag+ is absent in our biosensing environment. Meanwhile, the cofactor of DNAzyme, Mg2+, does not interfere the emission of carbon nanodots.

Step-by-step DNA assembly at the electrode interface is characterized by EIS and CV. The diameter of semicircle domain in a nyquist plot is the reflection of charge transfer resistance of the molecular layer at the electrode surface [32]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, bare electrode does not contain any semicircle domain, indicating excellent electrical conductivity. After modified with Probe A, a larger semicircle can be observed. The diameter of the domain increases gradually with further addition of ctDNA, Probe B and Probe C. The reason is that the employed DNA strands participate in the molecular assembly at the electrode surface in succession which repel electrochemical species and increase the charge transfer resistance. ΔG values for step-by-step reactions are then calculated, which demonstrate thermodynamic tendency theoretically (Fig. S5). The direct evidence of the reactions can be found in the polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, in which the sizes of reactants and products during assembly process are as expected (Fig. S6). After further introduction of cofactor Mg2+, the semicircle diameter of the nyquist plot decreases, which is due to the cleavage reaction mediated by the DNAzyme catalyzed reaction. CV scans are then performed. As shown in Fig. 2B, during the assembly process of DNA nanostructures, the current peak intensity gradually decreases, demonstrating the increased charge transfer resistance caused by the DNA strands. After the addition of Mg2+, the peak currents increase. The variation trend is consistent with EIS results.

We have evaluated the electrochemical performances of DNA modified electrode in the absence and presence of target by SWV. As shown in Fig. 3 A, no peak can be observed for bare electrode. After target-initiated hand-in-hand DNA monolayer assembly, MB located at the electrode attributes to the significant SWV peak. However, without the assistant of target, Probe B cannot interact with the other strands and the peak is negligible. Optimized experimental parameters should be studied for better performances. The effect of Probe A concentration on electrode modification (Probes A/B/C) is firstly evaluated by EIS. Larger amount of Probe A strands are supposed to be immobilized with larger concentration used. In the presence of target, more Probes B/C could be assembled. Thus, larger semicircle domain is resulted. However, after the concentration of Probe A reaches 800 nM, the interface of Probe A modified electrode becomes much crowded, which is not suitable for effective construction of hand-in-hand monolayer. Therefore, the semicircle domain is decreased and 800 nM is selected as the optimal concentration (Fig. S7A and S7B). Since Probe B is labeled with MB, which could be facilely determined be by the measurement of SWV peak. With the increase of Probe B and C, more strands with MB molecules can be located at the electrode and the peak current can be enlarged. Optimal concentration of 1.2 μM is applied for further experiments (Fig. S7C and S7D). Similarly, by comparing the SWV peaks, the shortest time for complete assembly of DNA monolayer is chosen to be 100 min (Fig. S7E and S7F).

Under these optimized parameters, we can sense target ctDNA quantitively. SWV curves are recorded in Fig. 3B. Larger levels of ctDNA can be used to trigger the formation of three-way junctions and more MB contributes to larger SWV peak. The relationship between peak intensity and logarithm of ctDNA concentration is established in Fig. 3C. The linear equation is as follows:

in which y is the peak current and x is the logarithm of ctDNA concentration. Compared with other reported assays, the performances are excellent (Table S3). We have then measured the fluorescence emission of solution after incubating the electrode in Mg2+ solution. Significant peak is observed in the emission spectrum for 1 pM target. In the absence of target, no Probe B and C can be immobilized and no carbon nanodots can be released into the solution. Therefore, no fluorescence peak appears (Fig. 3D). We have then introduced target ctDNA with a series of concentrations. The image of the solutions after the reaction is then captured by the large sight field fluorescence imager (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. 3E, the solution after reaction with more ctDNA becomes brighter, indicating more released carbon nanodots. The results of corresponding fluorescence emission spectra are in good agreement with the image. Detailed relationship between peak intensity variation (compared with bare carbon nanodots) and logarithm of ctDNA concentration is summarized in Fig. 3F. Dual-mode biosensing principle based on two simultaneous detection approaches is a promising concept. In addition, ratiometric analysis strategy shows the merits of self-calibration and improved reliability. We have further normalized the responses of SWV peak and fluorescence peak variation. The ratio of the two signals is then applied as a new index. It is clear that i/ΔF changes more significantly for larger levels of ctDNA, which is proved to be a good indicator for ctDNA information (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, selectivity and practical utility investigations
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of this approach is demonstrated by two interassays. We have employed four batches of carbon nanodots and four independent working electrodes for the determination of different concentrations of ctDNA. The average relative standard deviations of fluorescence and electrochemical responses are below 5 %, verifying excellent reproducibility (Fig. S10). We have prepared five mismatched sequences for selectivity assessments. SWV peak current, fluorescence response and i/ΔF are recorded and compared. As shown in Fig. 4 A to 4C, all the three responses for mismatched sequences can be successfully distinguished from target cases, demonstrating good selectivity of the proposed dual-mode analysis. To demonstrate potential utility in biological samples, different levels of ctDNA are spiked in human serum samples before DNA assembly and cleavage reactions. Electrochemical and fluorescent measurements are performed. The obtained i/ΔF values are consistent with those in PBS experiments, verifying that the proposed biosensor has great potential use for real sample analysis (Fig. 4D).

In summary, we present a facile sensing approach for ctDNA on the basis of target triggered assembly of hand-in-hand structured DNA monolayer. Three functional hairpin probes participate in the construction of three-way junctions, which could be linked together via swing arms and provide significant electrochemical response. The further addition of DNAzyme cofactor in turn breaks the linkages of the monolayer and the released carbon nanodots emit bright fluorescence to probe initial ctDNA. The dual modes are smartly integrated for accurate and highly sensitive determination of target DNA. Compared with other sensing strategies, this study not only develops a facile way for DNA monolayer construction with target recycles, but also provides dual responding signals for the detection of target, which are beneficial to practical use in cancer diagnosis. The design can also inspire future development of DNA nanostructures for the detection of various targets.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (XDA16021101), and the Basic Research Program of Suzhou (SJC2021016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.138069.

ce:section.ce:para:
